results.possibleIdentity: Frank Clifford Rose (also known as F. Clifford Rose) (1926–2012) was a prominent British neurologist who was deeply involved in the intersection of neuroscience and the arts. While primarily a scientist, he is historically significant for his work exploring the relationship between the brain and artistic creativity, a field that mirrors the goals of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He edited several influential volumes on this subject, including "Neurology of the Arts: Painting, Music, Literature" (2004) and "The Neurobiology of Painting" (2006). His work analyzed the neurological conditions of famous artists like Goya and Van Gogh and examined how the brain processes aesthetic experiences. He was active in London during the late 1960s and 1970s, qualifying as a doctor in 1949 and being appointed Consultant Neurologist at Charing Cross Hospital in 1965.

Clifford Rose (born John Clifford Rose; 1929–2021) was a renowned British stage and television actor. He was a founding member of the Royal Shakespeare Company (RSC) in 1960 and was later named an RSC Honorary Associate Artist. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, he was highly active in experimental and classical theater, notably appearing in Peter Brook's groundbreaking 1964 production of "Marat/Sade" as Monsieur Coulmier. His association with the RSC and his designation as an "Associate Artist" often place him in historical records alongside visual and performance artists. He is perhaps best known for his 1970s role as Ludwig Kessler in the BBC series "Secret Army."
